8 min read
I decided to break this module into two parts to make it more
useful and easier to consume. I strongly suggest that you complete
the work in Part I before moving on to Part II. It’s very easy to skip
ahead and that will not serve you well.
Take the time to get this part right and, when you’ve �nished this
work, what follows in Part II will be even better.
"You want to deliver to the world what you would buy if you were on
the other end." — Charlie Munger, USC Commencement Speech
Identifying audiences and creating offers that resonate with those
audiences are critically important skills. As I’ve mentioned
repeatedly, offers are how we monetize the attention we’re
buying with paid traf�c (or the attention we’re earning in other
ways).
However, in my experience, identifying audiences and creating
offers are among the most dif�cult work that we do.
The following is a concise, step by step method that will help.
Audience and Offer Masterclass
(Part I)
www.getwsodo.com

-- 1 of 10 --

I don’t say that lightly. This is the exact method I will use when I
create my own example offer for the �rst cohort of The Traf�c
Engine. Notice the tense of that sentence — "will use".
Why is that important? Because I want you to have the same
experience I have, and the only way to ensure that happens is for
me to identify the method, and then follow it myself.
I would be doing each of you a terrible disservice if this module
was a theoretical construct that didn’t survive contact with reality.
After this masterclass is complete André will publish it. Then, like
you, I’ll go through it step by step, exactly as I’ve created it. If
there are issues I’ll discover those (and will update the content
accordingly).
However, I want to be clear that I don’t expect to make any
changes. I have taught this process at the college level for years,
and these frameworks are, by far, the most valuable and impactful
lessons I’ve taught.
Also, I have used these frameworks for years with clients and I
know they work. This is the �rst time I’ve put them all together
into a comprehensive process for others to use, and I am very
excited to see your results (and mine).
So let’s get started…
To make this as valuable as possible for everyone, I am going to
www.getwsodo.com

-- 2 of 10 --

start from the assumption you don’t even have an idea for an
offer. Even if you do, consume all of the content (2-3 times), and
then start wherever is appropriate for your situation.
Part 1 — The Venn diagram
A really strong offer is a combination of:
▪ The creator’s interests, experience, and/or abilities;
▪ An audience that has a need for the outcomes created by
those elements; and
▪ A clearly articulated demonstration of the value — to the
audience — those elements can create.
Let’s look at each of these individually before we put them
together.
The �rst question to answer is:
▪ "what interests you?"
▪ What skills have you learned, what hobbies have you
pursued, what experiences have you had?
▪ How do you choose to spend your free time?
▪ What would your calendar, and credit card statements say
about your interests?
www.getwsodo.com

-- 3 of 10 --

I suggest that you make three lists:
▪ Skills.
▪ Hobbies/interests.
▪ Experience (professional and personal).
Do not rush through this — expect to spend 2-3 hours, minimum,
and to revisit the list over days (or weeks).
Once you have lists that feel reasonably complete, take one pass
through all of them and cross out (or delete) anything that doesn’t
really excite you. Then, start asking "why" — repeatedly — for
everything on your list (and capture your answers on a separate
list). I prefer to do this with pencil and paper — digital works too,
but in my experience it’s not as effective. (You will make a digital
list later.)
Don’t just ask "why" once and move one. Dig deeply. You’re
looking for the core drivers underneath your interests, hobbies,
and powerful experiences. Five Whys is the benchmark.
This process should take as long as it takes. If you want to �nish
in an afternoon that’s �ne. Or, if you want to spend a week or two,
that’s also �ne. There is no right answer, there’s only your answer.
At the end of this process you should have a signi�cantly shorter
list that’s a collection of themes that show up consistently in your
life.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 4 of 10 --

For example, when I’ve done this exercise a love of learning shows
up as a central theme in my life. That is foundational for me, and
it’s a powerful motivator. My love of learning also is a way I’m
able to contribute to others.
Next, when you look at that revised list of themes, start to
imagine who could bene�t from what you already know? It’s
very important to be speci�c in this exercise.
Let’s look at some examples.
I have been a black belt in Taekwondo since I was sixteen, and as
an assistant instructor I have developed a sparring curriculum for
students who want to improve their sparring skills in a non-
competitive environment. I’ve taught and re�ned that program
for years, and I know that I can show new students (and long-time
practitioners) ways to improve their recreational sparring skills
signi�cantly.
I have no interest in teaching that virtually, which means that the
audience that can bene�t from that are students who belong to
the same association I do, who are not interested in competition
sparring, and who have an interest in improving their recreational
sparring skills beyond what they learn during the regular class
curriculum. That subset of students is the ideal audience for a
skill I have developed and enjoy.
I also have experience as a digital marketing freelancer who built
www.getwsodo.com

-- 5 of 10 --

an agency, and I have continued to run an agency for decades. I’m
often asked for advice by other freelancers interested in making
that same transition, or who already have small agencies they
want to grow. That audience — digital marketing freelancers or
small agency owners — is an ideal match for skills and experience
I possess.
Now it’s your turn. Who, speci�cally, would bene�t from knowing
something that you already know? Speci�city is key. The more
focused you are on the audience the more powerful the offer will
be.
For example, if you’re an American-born, English-speaking digital
nomad who has lived and worked remotely in Paris for a year, and
you learned to speak conversational French after you arrived, your
experience and the lessons you’ve learned are valuable to anyone
who is considering a similar move.
Your insights would be most valuable to other English-speaking
Americans who have not yet learned to speak French. You would
know their speci�c concerns and could speak to those
authoritatively. That does not mean your insights wouldn’t be
valuable to others contemplating a move to Paris. However, your
insights would be most valuable to the subset of people perfectly
matched to your experience. That’s where you want to start.
After you’ve identi�ed speci�c potential audiences, the last step is
to get very clear about the value you could offer each audience.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 6 of 10 --

Using our Paris example above, make a list of all of the concerns
you had when you were contemplating the move to Paris.
For example:
▪ how did you �nd and lease an apartment without speaking
French?
▪ How did you �nd and sign up for French language
instruction?
▪ What have you learned about French requirements for
foreign workers?
▪ Where, how, and how much do you pay in taxes?
The list goes on and on. Every problem you’ve identi�ed and
solved, and every challenge you have overcome, is useful to your
audience who has not yet done the same. In addition, just
knowing what the problems are and where the challenges will be
is incredibly valuable.
Think deeply about where and how you can create value for a very
speci�c audience. Don’t worry about the mechanism for that —
stay focused on the value. An easy way to do this is imagining a
conversation with your ideal prospect (or actually having those
conversations).
▪ Who are they?
▪ What do they need that you can help with?
www.getwsodo.com

-- 7 of 10 --

▪ What questions do they ask?
▪ What questions don’t they ask that they should ask?
Capture all of that (analog or digital).
At the end of this process you’re going to have something
remarkable. You will have identi�ed, step by step, the intersection
of the potential value you can bring to the world, with a vision of a
speci�c audience that could bene�t from that value, and clarity
about exactly why and how that value is useful to that audience.
You may not get there on the �rst attempt and that’s OK. Keep
working at it. Once you have it you’ll know. It’ll feel right, and
you’ll most likely feel a combination of excitement and anxiety.
That’s a sign you’re on the right track.
One last step before we move on. Take this step very seriously and
do not skip it.
I want you to imagine it’s three years in the future and you have
successfully built (or expanded) a business from the ideas you
identi�ed in this exercise. You have matched your interests to an
audience, and have created real value for that audience.
▪ How does that feel?
▪ Be honest with yourself. Is that a business you would want?
▪ Is that a skill or ability you’d like to focus on most of the time
www.getwsodo.com

-- 8 of 10 --

in your professional life?
▪ Would you enjoy that work, day after day?
Often, especially the �rst time we do this exercise, the answer is
no. I know someone, for example, who loved brewing beer. He
spent his nights and weekends creating and testing recipes, and
after he graduated from college he opened a successful brewery.
Two years later the brewery closed — not because it failed (it was
successful �nancially) — but because it wasn’t what he wanted to
do. There’s a vast difference between brewing beer as a hobby and
brewing beer as a vocation.
Think about that when you’re doing this exercise. It’s easy to
imagine something won’t be successful — it’s equally important to
imagine that your idea is wildly successful and verify that’s
something you would want to be true for you. It’s a lot easier to
make changes now when we’re still in the idea phase, so choose
something that really lights you up.
Take your time with this part. Do the push ups because they’re
very important. In Part II we’re going to convert the work you’ve
done into a razor-sharp offer perfectly matched to the needs of an
audience, and then identify the most in�uential way to describe
your offer to that audience.
The time you invest in the exercises in part I will contribute
signi�cantly to your impact in part II.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 9 of 10 --

— Shawn
www.getwsodo.com

-- 10 of 10 --
